nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving forest streams, pools, and swampy areas with dense leaf litter and overhanging vegetation; soft, acidic, tannin-stained water under closed rainforest canopy

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Java moss, Cryptocoryne spp.)
floating plants to diffuse light and reduce jumping
leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, oak leaves) to acidify and tint water
driftwood and twisted roots for structure
dark substrate (fine dark sand or leaf-litter bed)
tight-fitting lid — mandatory given high escape risk
gentle filtration (sponge filter) to avoid strong currents

diet.notes: Primarily accepts small live and frozen invertebrates: fruit flies (Drosophila), micro worms, white worms, grindal worms, baby brine shrimp, daphnia, and bloodworms. Will usually accept high-quality small carnivore micro-pellets and freeze-dried foods over time, but variety with live/frozen items is strongly recommended for long-term condition and breeding. Feed small portions twice daily; overfeeding fouling small tanks must be avoided.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras) in larger tanks
corydoras (small species — C. habrosus, C. pygmaeus)
other small non-competitive killifish in a species-only setup
avoidWith: larger or aggressive fish that may intimidate or harass
fast-moving boisterous species that outcompete at feeding
other Aphyosemion males without ample territory (male conspecific aggression risk)
shrimp smaller than 2 cm (fry and juveniles may be eaten)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase thumbnail of male A. striatum showing characteristic red horizontal stripes on body and caudal fin extensions.
Wikimedia Commons male specimen photo; verify attribution and dimensions before use.

summary: A strikingly coloured non-annual killifish from Gabon’s rainforest streams, males displaying vivid red horizontal stripes on a turquoise-green body with lyretail fin extensions. Hardy relative to other killifish; suitable for nano planted tanks. Tight lid essential — a confirmed jumper.
careNotes: Aphyosemion striatum is one of the more forgiving Aphyosemion species and a recommended entry point for killifish keeping, though it still demands soft, slightly acidic water and attentive husbandry. Multiple locality forms (e.g., GA 1, GA 2, Libreville, Lastourville) exist, differing subtly in male coloration patterns; collectors often maintain these separately to preserve locality purity. A tight-fitting, gapless lid is non-negotiable — A. striatum is a strong jumper and will exploit any opening, including around equipment cables. A mature planted nano tank (40–60 L) with gentle sponge filtration, dim lighting filtered by floating plants, and a leaf-litter bed best replicates its tannin-stained rainforest stream habitat. Maintain soft water (gH 1–10, kH 1–5) and slightly acidic pH (6.0–7.0); peat filtration or Indian almond leaves can help condition tap water. Temperature should stay in the 20–26°C range — avoid overheating, which stresses the fish and shortens lifespan. Weekly partial water changes of 20–25% are important to prevent nitrate accumulation in small tanks. Males can be aggressive toward each other in confined spaces; a single pair or a trio (1 male, 2 females) is the safest arrangement in most aquaria. In larger, heavily planted tanks 2 pairs may coexist. This species is a non-annual killifish and does not require diapause or dry-season egg storage — eggs develop continuously in water.
breedingNotes: Aphyosemion striatum is an egg-scatterer that deposits adhesive eggs in fine-leaved plants (Java moss, spawning mops) or directly among leaf litter. A dedicated breeding tank of 20–30 L with a spawning mop or clump of Java moss is the standard approach. Water should be very soft and acidic (pH 6.0–6.5, gH 1–5). Eggs hatch in approximately 14–21 days at 22–24°C. The eggs are non-diapause (continuous development) and must not be dried. Fry are free-swimming immediately on hatching and accept infusoria, micro worms, and newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii from day one. Eggs and early fry should be separated from adults to prevent predation. Remove eggs daily by swapping spawning mops, rinsing in clean tank water, and incubating in a shallow dish or separate container. Broodstock conditioning with live foods accelerates spawning activity.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Aphyosemion striatum
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Gabon), water parameters (temperature 20–26°C, pH 6.0–7.0, hardness 18–90 ppm), adult size (~4 cm), habitat (shaded forest streams with tannin-stained water), diet (small live/frozen invertebrates), tank setup recommendations, breeding methodology, and locality form notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Aphyosemion striatum
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Nothobranchiidae, Cyprinodontiformes), adult size (to 5.0 cm TL), temperature range (20–26°C), native range (Gabon), habitat description, and diet (invertivore). Confirmed non-annual status.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Killifish Care Guide
additional.notes: Used as hobbyist trade perspective. Corroborated small tank size suitability (20–30+ gal not needed; 10 gal / ~40 L adequate for pairs), carnivore diet with live/frozen foods, escape risk (strong jumper, tight lid required), and intermediate care classification. Confirmed general non-annual group behaviour.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Killifish Species Guide
additional.notes: Provided context on Aphyosemion genus husbandry: soft acidic water, tannin staining, locality form keeping practices, non-annual egg-scattering breeding methodology, and recommended approach of species-only or quiet community setups. Confirmed 14–21 day egg incubation period at typical temperatures.

additional.name: American Killifish Association — Aphyosemion striatum species account
additional.notes: Specialist killifish society source. Provided locality form details (GA codes referencing Gabon collection sites), breeding mop technique, egg incubation in water vs. damp peat methodology, fry food recommendations (micro worms, BBS nauplii), and notes on male coloration variation between locality forms.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; deposits adhesive eggs in fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Non-diapause — eggs develop continuously in water (~14–21 days at 22–24°C). Separate adults from eggs to prevent predation. Fry accept micro worms and BBS nauplii from first feeding. Conditioning adults on live foods prior to spawning improves yield.
